content.text: Scaling laws are empirical power-law relationships that predict how a language model's validation loss changes as you vary model size, training data, and compute budget. Rather than guess-and-check, they let practitioners forecast performance before training and allocate resources near-optimally — transforming model development from art into a discipline grounded in measurement.
as a smooth, predictable function of three quantities:
— the number of training tokens consumed
— the total compute budget in floating-point operations (FLOPs)
The foundational form, introduced by Kaplan et al. (OpenAI, 2020) and refined by Hoffmann et al. (DeepMind Chinchilla, 2022), is:
is the irreducible entropy of the dataset — the theoretical lower bound no model can cross. The identity linking all three variables is approximately:

The factor of 6 accounts for the forward pass (2ND FLOPs) and the backward pass (approximately 4ND FLOPs) for a standard Transformer. This equality lets practitioners compute any one of N, D, or C given the other two.

Before scaling laws, choosing model size and training duration was largely intuitive. Teams would train several models, compare loss, and extrapolate — wasting enormous compute on configurations that were never near-optimal. Scaling laws change this:
— fit a scaling curve on small runs (1–5% of the budget) and extrapolate to the full scale with ~5% loss error.
— given a fixed GPU budget, find the joint (N, D) pair that minimises loss rather than training an oversized model on too few tokens.
— different architectural choices (attention variants, activation functions) can be ranked on small models and the winner promoted to full scale.
— capabilities that appear discontinuously at scale can be anticipated, even if not perfectly predicted, by tracking loss trajectories.
— Chinchilla-optimal training achieves the same loss as an over-parameterised baseline at 3–5× lower compute.
— power-law fits trained on runs as small as 10⁷ FLOPs can predict loss at 10²³ FLOPs within a few percent.
— scaling experiments are systematic: vary one axis, hold the rest fixed, measure loss, repeat. The methodology is replicable.
— models trained on more tokens per parameter are smaller at inference time for the same loss, cutting serving costs substantially.
— small-scale ablations (architecture, data mix, tokeniser) transfer reliably to large-scale outcomes.
— running a grid of pilot experiments to fit (A, α, B, β, E) costs compute, though far less than a mis-sized full run.
— scaling laws are fit on a specific data distribution. Changing the data mix or tokeniser resets the constants.
— scaling laws predict cross-entropy loss, not benchmark accuracy. Some capabilities emerge sharply at thresholds that are hard to anticipate from loss alone.

— Chinchilla optimises training FLOPs. If inference cost matters (e.g., serving millions of queries), a smaller model trained on more tokens (like LLaMA) may be preferable even at higher training cost.
— the 6ND formula assumes standard dense Transformers; mixture-of-experts and speculative decoding change the accounting.
The Kaplan et al. Power Laws

The 2020 OpenAI paper ran hundreds of training runs across six orders of magnitude in compute. They found three independent power laws when holding the other variable abundant: loss scales as N^(−0.076) with parameters, D^(−0.095) with tokens, and C^(−0.050) with compute. Crucially, they concluded that, given a fixed compute budget, it is better to train a larger model on fewer tokens. This led to GPT-3 (175 B params, 300 B tokens) — which, in hindsight, was undertrained.

Hoffmann et al. (2022) repeated the experiment more carefully, varying both N and D simultaneously at fixed C. They fit iso-compute (IsoFLOP) profiles — curves of loss versus N with D = C / (6N) — and located the minimum of each. The envelope of these minima is the

. Their key finding: optimal N and optimal D scale proportionally with C^0.5, meaning parameters and tokens should grow at the same rate. The practical rule of thumb is approximately 20 training tokens per non-embedding parameter. Chinchilla (70 B params, 1.4 T tokens) matched or exceeded Gopher (280 B, 300 B tokens) across most benchmarks at one-quarter the size.
Compute-optimal training: for every doubling of compute, double both model size and dataset size. Neither axis should be neglected.

Think of the loss landscape as a two-dimensional valley plotted on log-log axes with N on the x-axis and L on the y-axis. For each fixed compute budget C, the constraint D = C/(6N) traces a curve across this valley — a curve with a single minimum. As C grows, the valley floor drops and shifts to larger N. The compute-optimal frontier is the locus of those minima.
Kaplan's mistake was looking only at the left slope of the valley (large N, small D), where more parameters always help. Chinchilla looked at both slopes and found the bottom.

A second mental model: think of N and D as two tanks filling a pool (the model's knowledge). If you fill one tank faster than the other, the pool overflows on one side. Chinchilla says fill both tanks at the same rate when your goal is to minimise total water used (compute) for a target pool level (loss).

attrs.alt: Scaling laws diagram: iso-compute loss contours and compute-optimal frontier on log-log axes of model parameters vs validation loss

content.text: Each dashed curve is an iso-compute contour: the loss achieved by models of different sizes trained to exhaust a fixed compute budget. The green line is the compute-optimal frontier — the locus of (N, D) pairs that minimise loss for each budget. Amber dots mark the optimal allocation per contour. Points to the left of each dot are undertrained (oversized models, too few tokens); points to the right are data-limited (undersized models, token-rich training).
Scaling laws are most useful in the following scenarios:
— the primary application. Use scaling curves to pick N and D before committing GPU months.
— refit the curve on domain data to predict how many domain tokens are needed to reach target loss.
— compare two architectural choices by training each over a range of small-scale runs and extrapolating to full scale.
— measure how filtering or deduplication shifts the loss curve; a lower constant B signals a higher-quality corpus.
— given a target loss, use the inverse formula to compute the required C, then translate that into GPU hours and budget.
directly useful for fine-tuning on small task-specific datasets (the domain of PEFT and few-shot tuning), or for predicting specific benchmark accuracy from loss.
Implementing a scaling law study involves three steps: running pilot experiments, fitting the functional form, and extrapolating to guide full-scale decisions.
Step 1 — Fit scaling parameters from pilot runs

import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ── Empirical scaling data (model_params, training_tokens, val_loss) ─────────
# Each tuple: (N in params, D in tokens, L measured)
observations = [
    (1e7,  2e8,  3.85),
    (1e8,  2e9,  2.90),
    (5e8,  1e10, 2.41),
    (1e9,  2e10, 2.20),
    (7e9,  1.4e11, 1.87),
    (7e10, 1.4e12, 1.64),
]

N_arr = np.array([o[0] for o in observations])
D_arr = np.array([o[1] for o in observations])
L_arr = np.array([o[2] for o in observations])

# ── Model: L(N, D) = A/N^alpha + B/D^beta + E ────────────────────────────────
def scaling_loss(X, A, alpha, B, beta, E):
    N, D = X
    return A / N**alpha + B / D**beta + E

X_data = (N_arr, D_arr)
p0 = [406.4, 0.34, 410.7, 0.28, 1.69]   # Chinchilla paper initial guesses

popt, pcov = curve_fit(
    scaling_loss, X_data, L_arr, p0=p0,
    bounds=([0]*5, [np.inf]*5), maxfev=10_000
)

A, alpha, B, beta, E = popt
print(f"Fitted parameters:")
print(f"  A={A:.3f}  alpha={alpha:.4f}")
print(f"  B={B:.3f}  beta={beta:.4f}")
print(f"  E (irreducible)={E:.4f}")

# ── Predict loss for a new model ─────────────────────────────────────────────
N_new, D_new = 13e9, 3e11      # 13 B params, 300 B tokens
L_pred = scaling_loss((N_new, D_new), *popt)
print(f"\nPredicted loss for N={N_new:.0e}, D={D_new:.0e}: {L_pred:.3f}")

import math

# ── Chinchilla optimal allocation ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# Given compute budget C (FLOPs), the optimal N* and D* satisfy:
#   N* ~ (C / 6)^0.5   (from Hoffmann et al. 2022, coefficient ~0.5)
#   D* = C / (6 * N*)
# Rule of thumb: ~20 training tokens per parameter.

def chinchilla_optimal(compute_flops: float, tokens_per_param: float = 20.0):
    # Return (N_opt, D_opt) for a given compute budget C (FLOPs).
    # C = 6 * N * D  =>  with D = tpp * N:  C = 6 * tpp * N^2
    # => N = sqrt(C / (6 * tpp))
    # From C = 6 * N * D and D = tokens_per_param * N:
    #   C = 6 * N * (tpp * N) = 6 * tpp * N^2
    #   => N = sqrt(C / (6 * tpp))
    N_opt = math.sqrt(compute_flops / (6 * tokens_per_param))
    D_opt = tokens_per_param * N_opt
    return N_opt, D_opt

# ── Example: A100 GPU budget ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
GPU_FLOPS = 3.12e14          # A100 bfloat16 peak TFLOPs/s in FLOPs/s
GPU_UTIL  = 0.4              # realistic MFU for large runs
GPU_COUNT = 512
HOURS     = 720              # 30 days

C = GPU_FLOPS * GPU_UTIL * GPU_COUNT * HOURS * 3600
print(f"Compute budget: {C:.3e} FLOPs")

N_opt, D_opt = chinchilla_optimal(C)
print(f"Chinchilla-optimal model: {N_opt/1e9:.2f}B params")
print(f"Chinchilla-optimal data:  {D_opt/1e9:.0f}B tokens")
print(f"Ratio D/N: {D_opt/N_opt:.1f} tokens per param")

# ── Sweep: how optimal N scales with C ───────────────────────────────────────
print("\nCompute budget  =>  Optimal model size")
for exp in range(20, 26):
    C_i = 10 ** exp
    N_i, _ = chinchilla_optimal(C_i)
    print(f"  C = 10^{exp}  =>  N* = {N_i/1e9:.2f}B params")
Step 3 — Plot IsoFLOP profiles to inspect the loss landscape

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ── IsoFLOP profiles: trace loss vs N at fixed C ─────────────────────────────
# Uses the Chinchilla functional form (Hoffmann et al. 2022, Appendix D).

A, alpha = 406.4, 0.34
B, beta  = 410.7, 0.28
E        = 1.69

def loss(N, D):
    return A / N**alpha + B / D**beta + E

def isoflop_sweep(C, n_points=200):
    # For fixed compute C, sweep N and set D = C/(6N).
    # N must be at least 1 and D at least 1; also avoid D < 1 token
    N_min = max(1e6, C / (6 * 1e12))   # D <= 1 T tokens
    N_max = min(1e12, C / (6 * 1e6))   # D >= 1 M tokens
    N_vals = np.logspace(np.log10(N_min), np.log10(N_max), n_points)
    D_vals = C / (6 * N_vals)
    L_vals = loss(N_vals, D_vals)
    return N_vals, L_vals

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
budgets = {
    "C = 10²¹": 1e21,
    "C = 10²²": 1e22,
    "C = 10²³": 1e23,
}
colors = ["#38bdf8", "#34d399", "#f59e0b"]
opt_Ns, opt_Ls = [], []
for (label, C), color in zip(budgets.items(), colors):
    N_vals, L_vals = isoflop_sweep(C)
    ax.plot(N_vals, L_vals, color=color, label=label)
    idx = np.argmin(L_vals)
    opt_Ns.append(N_vals[idx])
    opt_Ls.append(L_vals[idx])
    ax.scatter([N_vals[idx]], [L_vals[idx]], color=color, s=60, zorder=5)

ax.plot(opt_Ns, opt_Ls, "w--", lw=1.5, label="Compute-optimal frontier")
ax.set_xscale("log"); ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Model parameters N"); ax.set_ylabel("Validation loss L")
ax.set_title("IsoFLOP profiles — Chinchilla functional form")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("isoflop_profiles.png", dpi=150)
print("Saved isoflop_profiles.png")
Run pilot experiments covering at least two decades in N (e.g., 10 M to 1 B parameters) and two decades in D, training each model to convergence. Fit the five-parameter curve with
using Chinchilla's published values as initial guesses (A=406.4, α=0.34, B=410.7, β=0.28, E=1.69). Then apply the Chinchilla formula to your compute budget.
The following models illustrate the progression from Kaplan-style to Chinchilla-optimal training:
— 175 B parameters, ~300 B tokens, ~3.1 × 10²³ FLOPs. Kaplan-optimal for its era: larger model, relatively few tokens. Chinchilla analysis later showed it was substantially undertrained.
— 70 B parameters, 1.4 T tokens, same compute budget as Gopher (280 B / 300 B). Matched or beat Gopher on most tasks at one-quarter the inference cost.

— 7 B to 65 B parameters, 1 T to 1.4 T tokens. Deliberately "inference-optimal": trained on more tokens than Chinchilla-optimal so the smallest model achieves GPT-3-level performance at much lower serving cost.
— 8 B model trained on 15 T tokens — roughly 1 875 tokens per parameter, far beyond Chinchilla-optimal, reflecting that inference cost now dominates training cost in production deployments.
Suppose a team has a budget of 512 A100 GPUs for 30 days, approximately 1.7 × 10²³ FLOPs. Running the Chinchilla formula:

This is the training-compute-optimal point. If the model will serve millions of requests, the team may choose a 13 B model on 1.3 T tokens (same compute, more tokens per param) to halve inference cost, accepting a small loss penalty. The decision framework below compares the options:
Training a large model on few tokens
— the classic pre-Chinchilla error. A 70 B model on 300 B tokens is expensive at inference and worse in loss than a 35 B model on 600 B tokens using the same FLOPs.
— when fitting curves, omitting the floor constant E causes the exponents to absorb its effect and biases extrapolations at large N or D.
— a scaling law needs at least a 2D grid of (N, D) pairs. A single training curve of one model versus steps is useful but insufficient to fit the joint function.
Assuming the law transfers across data distributions
— the fitted constants A, B, α, β depend on the tokeniser and data mix. Changing either invalidates the fit.
— Chinchilla finds the best allocation of a fixed budget. If you can train longer or on better data, the optimal point shifts.
— for sparse models (MoE), the effective N is the number of active parameters per token, not the total parameter count. The formula changes accordingly.
— for a new architecture or data mix, train 10–15 small models at fixed C, varying N from 10 M to 1 B, to locate the minimum before committing to scale.
— work with log(L), log(N), log(D) for fitting. Power laws are linear in log-log space; this conditions the optimiser better.
Hold out a validation set from the target distribution
— the loss you minimise should match the loss you care about. Don't fit on train loss.
— always publish all three. A model's loss only makes sense in the context of its compute budget.
— 20 tokens/param is training-compute-optimal; 50–100+ tokens/param is inference-optimal. Decide which matters for your deployment before training.
Validate the fit at held-out scales
— before using the law to guide a 10²³ FLOP run, verify that it correctly predicts a 10²² FLOP run that you held out of the fit.
Scaling law experiments themselves must be efficient. Several practices reduce cost:
Early stopping with learning rate schedule
— use a cosine schedule with a final LR of 10% of peak. Comparing loss at the end of the schedule (not mid-run) is required for valid loss comparisons across N.
— short runs (< 1 B tokens) are particularly sensitive to warmup length. Standardise it as a fraction of total training steps.
— when running many small models in parallel, gradient checkpointing reduces VRAM to allow more parallel runs per node.
— use data parallelism for pilot runs since model parallelism adds communication overhead that distorts wall-clock efficiency.
— always measure Model FLOP Utilisation. The C = 6ND formula counts theoretical FLOPs; real utilisation (typically 35–55% on A100) means actual wall-clock time is
— minimal GPT-2 trainer ideal for running scaling experiments on a single machine; easy to instrument for loss measurement.
— production-grade large-model trainer with built-in FLOP counting and MFU reporting; used by many scaling law papers.
— config-driven trainer with compute budget arguments; supports Chinchilla-optimal token budgets out of the box.
— lightweight, hackable trainer; good for ablation studies and custom architectures.
— standard tool for fitting the power-law functional form; handles non-linear least squares with bounds.
— experiment tracking essential for managing a grid of pilot runs and visualising scaling curves interactively.
— standardised benchmark suite; use it to verify that loss improvements from scaling translate to capability gains.
Loss vs N, D, C separately
Joint optimal (N*, D*) at fixed C
Optimises training cost, not inference cost
Optimal N for serving, given token budget
Lower per-query cost at production scale
Full loss surface at fixed C
Reveals valley shape; catches non-power-law regimes
Expensive to collect; needs 10+ runs per budget
Best architecture at fixed parameter count
Can find gains orthogonal to scaling
Does not predict absolute loss at large scale
Scaling law studies influence decisions worth millions of dollars of compute. Several reliability considerations apply:
— with five free parameters and few data points, the curve can overfit. Use at least 8–12 (N, D) configurations and hold out two for validation before trusting extrapolations.
— if the validation set overlaps with training data, loss is optimistically biased. The fitted law then underestimates real loss at scale. Deduplicate rigorously before measuring.
— report loss to three decimal places with standard deviation across seeds. Tiny loss differences drive large extrapolation errors due to the power-law exponent.
— BF16 vs FP32 and different CUDA kernels shift loss by up to 0.02 nats. Fit and extrapolate within the same hardware and precision regime.

— scaling laws describe cross-entropy loss on the pre-training distribution. Do not use them to predict safety properties, hallucination rates, or adversarial robustness — these do not scale predictably from loss alone.
in N, D, and C — a fact stable across six orders of magnitude in compute.
: optimal training requires scaling tokens at the same rate as parameters, not prioritising model size.
is ~20 tokens per non-embedding parameter for compute-optimal training; production teams often use 50–200× for inference efficiency.
Pilot experiments on 1–5% of the budget
can predict full-run loss within a few percent — making scaling law studies among the highest-ROI activities in large-model development.
— the right token-to-param ratio depends on whether training cost or serving cost dominates your economics.
The constants (A, α, B, β, E) are distribution-specific
— changing tokeniser, data mix, or model architecture resets the fit.
Scaling laws predict loss, not capabilities
— emergent abilities can appear discontinuously and are not directly forecast by the power-law form.
